Blog/Marketing Research
Marketing Research — BUSM 304

Real Data. Real Insights. Real Marketing.

Meredith T. Villaluz Spring 20265 min read

This week in my Principles of Marketing class at BYU–Hawaii, I simultaneously read Chapters 3 and 4 of the Kotler textbook and completed a comprehensive secondary data analysis assignment in Excel. Recognizing that both activities conveyed the same core concepts significantly deepened my understanding.

What the Textbook Taught Me

Chapter 3 examines the macroenvironment — focusing on external forces such as demographic shifts, economic conditions, and cultural change that influence marketing decisions. Chapter 4 emphasizes that structured marketing research, particularly the transformation of raw secondary data into actionable customer insights, is essential for navigating this environment.

What the Data Revealed

I analyzed a dataset of 295 customers, examining their incomes, spending habits, and demographic characteristics. Here's what I found:

Customer Dataset — Key Findings (n = 295)
49.8
Average Customer Age
35.6%
Largest Group: Ages 49–54
$116K
Average Annual Income
$203.67
Avg Monthly Spend
56.3%
Male Customers
3.1/5
Brand Rating (100K+ income)

How I Built the Analysis

To generate these insights, I constructed pivot tables using COUNTIF and COUNTIFS formulas, created pie charts for gender and marital status, developed a horizontal bar chart for education, and grouped customers into five age bands — all referencing the original data sheet.

"Marketing isn't guesswork — it's patterns hiding in data, waiting to be found."

Chapter 4 asserts that marketers should integrate traditional research methods with digital tools to generate actionable insights for strategic decision-making. This assignment offered practical experience in applying these principles, as each formula and chart addressed specific marketing questions regarding the customer base.
